text: Arm number three of the robotic system is used as a liver retractor to expose the hiatus. The surgeon then reduces the parasophageal hernia using a double fenestrated grasper in arm number two, minimizing tissue compression to prevent tearing along the stomach and hiatus. The hernia sac is dissected in the next step.

The assistant holds the reduced stomach in the abdomen while the surgeon uses an advanced bipolar device to open the hernia sac. Dissection begins from the left side of the hiatus, with the sac being circumferentially separated from mediastinal and pleural attachments. The surgeon takes special care to avoid entering the pleural cavity, aided by three-dimensional visualization for better identification of tissue planes.

The surgeon mobilizes the hiatus by retracting the stomach down and laterally. The frontal esophageal ligament is divided using an advanced bipolar device. Dissection continues around the gastroesophageal junction, creating a retroesophageal window with gentle blunt dissection. A Penrose drain encircles the esophagus for non-traumatic retraction.
The surgeon completes the division of the phrenoesophageal ligament and proceeds to circumferentially mobilize the distal esophagus, ensuring an adequate intra-abdominal esophageal length.

The surgeon begins the hernia closure by placing interrupted zero silk stitches posteriorly on the hiatus, using a calibration bougie to ensure the closure is not too tight. Each suture is placed one centimeter apart, with bioabsorbable pledges included for reinforcement. The slipped knot technique is employed, following standard suturing principles used in regular laparoscopy.

The surgeon uses regular laparoscopic scissors to cut the anterior stitch deemed too constricting. An additional suture is placed posteriorly to adjust the closure. Four robotic instruments are utilized throughout the procedure, including the Cartier Grasper, Double Fenestrated Grasper, Needle Driver, and Bipolar Cutting Device.

An intraoperative endoscopy is performed to verify the easy passage of the endoscope, assess the position of the gastroesophageal junction (GEJ), check the intraabdominal esophageal length, and evaluate the mucosal integrity. A pneumatic leak test is demonstrated, confirming the absence of fundoplication due to ineffective esophageal motility.

A round-blade drain is placed in the mediastinum. The surgeon performs a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy, followed by closure of the port sites using standard techniques. The visual content concludes with the patient being prepared for discharge.
